cargo_changes:
kernel/Cargo.toml and crates/tb-hal/Cargo.toml: NONE. `alloc` is a toolchain crate, not a crates.io dependency, so no `[dependencies]` entry is added anywhere; tb-hal pulls `GlobalAlloc`/`Layout` from `core::alloc` (no dep), and the kernel reaches `alloc` via `extern crate alloc;`.

HOWEVER there is ONE required build change, in tabos/.cargo/config.toml (the `kbuild` alias — the single source of truth for build flags), which I made: `alloc` MUST be added to the `-Zbuild-std` list. On a none-class target there is no precompiled `alloc`; with the old `-Zbuild-std=core,compiler_builtins`, `extern crate alloc;` fails with "can't find crate for `alloc`". The alias now reads:

  kbuild = "build -p tabos-kernel -Zbuild-std=core,compiler_builtins,alloc -Zbuild-std-features=compiler-builtins-mem -Zjson-target-spec"

(only `core,compiler_builtins` -> `core,compiler_builtins,alloc`; everything else unchanged). This keeps the two `cargo kbuild --target ...` invocations and both boot paths working. No change to rust-toolchain.toml, the target JSON specs, or the run scripts.
feature_attrs:
NONE — no `#![feature(...)]` is required in either crate, on the pinned nightly (the sandbox reports a 1.92.0-class build).

- `extern crate alloc;` in a `#![no_std]` binary needs no feature on nightly (and on stable).
- A custom `#[alloc_error_handler]` is NOT defined, so `#![feature(alloc_error_handler)]` is unnecessary: the default alloc-error handler was stabilized in Rust 1.68 (the former `default_alloc_error_handler` feature), and it routes an allocation failure to the kernel's existing `#[panic_handler]`. Since the allocator returns null on OOM (never panicking inside `alloc`), this path is only reached if a caller actually exhausts the 2 MiB arena.
- tb-hal uses only stable-in-core items (`core::alloc::{GlobalAlloc, Layout}`, `UnsafeCell`, `AtomicBool`, `core::ptr`, `core::hint::spin_loop`), and `unsafe impl GlobalAlloc` / `unsafe impl Sync` need no feature.

The real `cargo kbuild` (nightly) is the final arbiter; nightly is not installed in this sandbox, but the load-bearing pieces were verified another way (see rationale).
linker_changes:
NONE — .bss already covers the arena on both arches; no script edit.

- kernel/linker/x86_64.ld: the `.bss (NOLOAD)` output section matches `*(.bss .bss.*)` between `__bss_start = .;` and `__bss_end = .;`. The zero-initialised `static ARENA: Arena` lands in `.bss.*`, so it sits inside [__bss_start, __bss_end) and the `_start` .bss-clear zeroes it (harmless — the arena starts zeroed and `heap_init` lays the free block down later in `rust_main`). Crucially the boot page tables and boot stack are placed BEFORE `__bss_start` (`*(.boot.pagetables) *(.boot.stack)` precede `__bss_start = .;`), so the 2 MiB arena cannot collide with the live stack/CR3 tables. `.bss` is NOLOAD/NOBITS, so 2 MiB adds nothing to the image file.
- kernel/linker/aarch64.ld: same shape — `__bss_start = .;` then `.bss (NOLOAD) { *(.bss .bss.*) ... __boot_stack ... }` then `__bss_end = .;`. The arena matches `*(.bss .bss.*)` and is covered by __bss_start/__bss_end (zeroed by `_start`). It merely advances `.` by 2 MiB, pushing `__boot_stack_top` 2 MiB higher; there is no fixed-size or fixed-address constraint to violate, and guest RAM (256 MiB, kernel at 0x4008_0000) has ample room.

Both scripts therefore place .bss with room and their __bss_start/__bss_end already span the arena; M5 is arch-neutral (a .bss arena needs no per-arch code), confirmed — no per-arch files were touched.
rationale:
ALGORITHM. An intrusive, address-ordered free-list with two-sided coalescing — deliberately NOT a bump allocator (a bump cannot reuse freed memory and would fail the DoD's reuse + no-leak checks). Each free region carries an in-place `FreeBlock { size, next }` header (16 B, align 8); the list is sorted by ascending address. `init` lays one free block over the whole arena. `alloc` is first-fit with front/back splitting; `dealloc` re-inserts and merges with adjacent free neighbours.

ALIGNMENT. `required(layout)` raises the request to `align = max(layout.align(), HEADER_ALIGN)` and `size = align_up(max(layout.size(), MIN_BLOCK), HEADER_ALIGN)`, so every region boundary stays header-aligned and an over-aligned request (align can far exceed the 8-byte header alignment) is honoured. `fit` computes the aligned payload start inside a candidate block; if the resulting FRONT gap is nonzero but smaller than a header (e.g. an 8-byte gap before a 16-aligned payload), it advances the start by one alignment step so the gap grows to >= MIN_BLOCK and can itself become a free block. The BACK gap must likewise be 0 or >= MIN_BLOCK. Because front/back slack is split off and returned to the list, the region handed to the caller is EXACTLY [ptr, ptr+size) — which is what lets `dealloc` reconstruct the region from `ptr` + the identical `Layout` with no stored boundary tag.

COALESCING. `insert_free_region` walks to the address-ordered insertion point, writes the header, then merges forward (node+cur if `addr+size == cur`) and backward (prev+node if `prev_end == addr`). This is what makes freed memory genuinely reusable: after a round of allocations is dropped, the arena re-coalesces toward a single block, so a later equal-shaped request gets the same address (the reuse proof) and a 128 B request can land where two freed 64 B blocks merged (the coalescing proof).

ZST / size==0. The GlobalAlloc contract says callers pass nonzero size (and Box/Vec never call the allocator for ZSTs — they use a dangling pointer), but `required` is defensive: it floors size at MIN_BLOCK, collapsing size==0 onto a real, symmetric alloc/dealloc region rather than fabricating a dangling pointer that `dealloc` could not account for. The host harness verifies a size==0 alloc yields a usable region and frees back to baseline.

OOM RETURNS NULL. `alloc` never panics: if no block fits, or if any size/alignment arithmetic would overflow (`align_up_checked`, `checked_add` in `fit`/`required`), it returns null — the GlobalAlloc failure signal. Accounting is only touched on success, so a failed (including over-arena) request leaves `used` unchanged.

SYNC / SINGLE-CORE SAFETY. The `#[global_allocator]` static must be `Sync`, but `GlobalAlloc::alloc` takes `&self`. Mirroring M2's `TaskStack`, the mutable `HeapState` lives in `HeapCell { UnsafeCell<HeapState>, AtomicBool }` with `unsafe impl Sync`; `with()` CASes the AtomicBool, then mints the single `&mut HeapState` for the closure, then releases. The kernel-facing `Heap` is a ZST forwarding to the crate-internal `GLOBAL_HEAP`, so the unsafe `impl GlobalAlloc`/`impl Sync` and the arena all stay in tb-hal (framekernel rule); the kernel just names `Heap` and uses safe fns. At M5 there is no prior heap and no interrupts (M8 brings the first timer IRQ), so execution is single-threaded and non-preemptible: the guard only sees uncontended acquires and the allocator never nests an allocation, so it cannot re-enter. The module doc and `with()` comment record this single-core assumption explicitly and flag that M8 must add interrupt-masking around the critical section (an IRQ that allocates mid-update would otherwise deadlock on the guard). Reuse-unchanged-by-M7 is honoured: the algebra is store-agnostic; only `ARENA` + the `init()` call change later.

SELF-TEST COVERAGE + NO-LEAK ASSERTION. The kernel self-test drives the GLOBAL allocator through real alloc types: Box (single alloc + deref), Vec<u32> pushed 1024 times (capacity starts at 0, so it grows past its first realloc several times via GlobalAlloc's default realloc; all elements verified across the moves), BTreeMap with 512 ordered insert/lookup pairs (many node allocations), and a String grown by repeated push_str (768 bytes). All are dropped, then it asserts `heap_used_bytes() == base_used` (no leak) and `heap_high_water() > base_used` (the heap really was exercised). A reuse probe Boxes-drops-Boxes and asserts the two addresses match (freed block reused) using only safe ref->*const->usize casts (no `unsafe`). Finally it calls `tb_hal::heap_selftest()`, which performs the raw-pointer checks the kernel cannot: an over-arena request returns null (handled, not UB) with accounting unchanged; an align-64 alloc is alignment-correct, writable across its length, freed-then-reallocated to the SAME address; and two adjacent freed blocks re-serve as one larger block (coalescing) — every step landing back at baseline. On success it prints exactly "M5: alloc OK\n" (the DoD marker); any failure prints "M5: FAIL <why>" and halts.

VERIFICATION PERFORMED (nightly `cargo kbuild` not available in this sandbox — only a standalone stable 1.92 with no rustup/nightly and no `-Z` support). To make the build the arbiter for the load-bearing parts I: (1) compiled the EXACT allocator algebra (FreeBlock/align/fit/insert/alloc/dealloc/accounting, verbatim) against a host-backed arena and RAN it — alignment, write/read, dealloc-to-baseline, front/payload/back re-coalescing to one node, same-address reuse, two-block coalescing into a 128 B serve, OOM->null with untouched accounting, size==0, and a 2000-op randomized mixed alloc/free stress (random sizes 1..4096, aligns 1..64, all writes in-bounds) that returns to baseline and fully re-coalesces — ALL PASS (MIN_BLOCK=16, HEADER_ALIGN=8); (2) type-checked the real `heap.rs` via `--emit=metadata` — zero errors/warnings; (3) compiled a faithful mirror of the kernel M5 block under `-D unsafe_code` — it builds and runs clean, proving the block needs NO `unsafe {}` (the framekernel rule holds, and `(&*probe) as *const u64 as usize` is safe). M0-M4 are untouched; M5 is appended after the M4 block and before the final `halt()`.
